era_context: The early 16th century: the Protestant Reformation is spreading rapidly in Northern Europe; the Ottoman Empire is consolidating power under Süleyman I; the Mughal Empire is being established in South Asia; the Spanish conquest of the Americas is consolidating; and the Renaissance is flourishing in Italy.

events.title: Siege of Rhodes (Ottoman conquest)
events.description: In 1522, Ottoman Sultan Süleyman I launched a major military campaign against the island of Rhodes, which was held by the Knights Hospitaller. The siege lasted from June to December 1522, and the Knights were forced to surrender and evacuate the island. The Ottoman conquest of Rhodes eliminated a major Christian stronghold in the eastern Mediterranean and demonstrated Ottoman naval and military superiority. The Knights Hospitaller relocated to Malta, where they would establish a new base.
key_figures: Grand Master Philippe Villiers de l'Isle-Adam (Knights Hospitaller)

sources.name: Ottoman chronicles and military records
Knights Hospitaller records and European diplomatic correspondence
Contemporary European chronicles

events.certainty_note: The Ottoman siege of Rhodes is well-documented in Ottoman, European, and Knights Hospitaller sources. The siege lasted from June to December 1522.
cross_references: Part of Süleyman I's expansion strategy
Led to the Knights Hospitaller's relocation to Malta

events.title: Edict of Worms enforcement and religious conflict
events.description: In 1522, the Edict of Worms, which had condemned Martin Luther and the Protestant Reformation, was being enforced (or attempted to be enforced) throughout the Holy Roman Empire. However, many German princes and cities were ignoring or circumventing the edict, providing protection to Protestant reformers and allowing the spread of Protestant theology. The year 1522 represents a critical moment in the early Reformation, when the religious and political authority of the Emperor and the Pope was being challenged.

sources.name: Imperial decrees and official records
Luther's writings and correspondence
Contemporary correspondence and diplomatic reports

events.certainty_note: The enforcement (or non-enforcement) of the Edict of Worms in 1522 is well-documented in imperial, papal, and German sources.
cross_references: Follows from the Edict of Worms (1521)
Led to the Diet of Nuremberg (1523)

events.title: Magellan's expedition reaches the Philippines
events.description: In 1522, the expedition led by Ferdinand Magellan (who had died in 1521) reached the Philippines and the Spice Islands. The expedition, which had left Spain in 1519, was attempting to circumnavigate the globe and to find a westward route to the Spice Islands. Though Magellan himself died in the Philippines in 1521, his expedition continued under the command of Juan Sebastián Elcano. The expedition's arrival in the Philippines marked a major achievement in European exploration and established Spanish claims to the region.
key_figures: Ferdinand Magellan (Portuguese explorer sailing for Spain, died 1521)
Juan Sebastián Elcano (Spanish navigator, expedition commander after Magellan's death)

sources.name: Antonio Pigafetta's account of the voyage
Spanish royal records and expedition documents
Later European historiography and compilations of exploration narratives

events.certainty_note: Magellan's expedition's arrival in the Philippines is well-documented in Antonio Pigafetta's account and Spanish royal records. The date of 1521 for Magellan's death and 1522 for the expedition's continued exploration is well-established.
cross_references: Part of early European exploration of the Pacific
Led to Spanish colonization of the Philippines

events.title: Babur's invasion of India continues
events.description: In 1522, Babur, the founder of the Mughal Empire, was conducting military campaigns in northern India. Babur had invaded India in 1519 and was gradually consolidating his power. The year 1522 represents a stage in his gradual conquest of northern India, which would culminate in the Battle of Panipat in 1526 and the establishment of the Mughal Empire.

sources.name: Babur-nama (Memoirs of Babur)
Mughal court records and later imperial histories

events.certainty_note: Babur's campaigns in 1522 are mentioned in his memoirs, though the specific details and chronology are sometimes uncertain.
cross_references: Part of the founding of the Mughal Empire
Led to the Battle of Panipat (1526)

events.title: Zhengde Emperor's reign continues
events.description: The Zhengde Emperor of the Ming Dynasty continued his reign in 1522, characterized by personal extravagance, delegation of power to eunuchs, and military campaigns against Mongol invasions. The Zhengde Emperor was known for his unconventional behavior and his reliance on eunuchs, which created tension with Confucian officials. His reign would end with his death in late 1525 or early 1526.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)
Official Ming court records and imperial edicts

events.certainty_note: The Zhengde Emperor's reign is well-documented in Ming imperial records.
cross_references: Led to the Jiajing Emperor's accession (1526)

events.title: Henry VIII's court and religious politics
events.description: In 1522, King Henry VIII of England was still married to Catherine of Aragon and had not yet begun formal proceedings for an annulment. However, the question of succession (Catherine had not produced a male heir) was becoming increasingly urgent. The year 1522 represents a transitional moment before the formal break with Rome that would begin in 1526.

sources.name: English royal records and court correspondence
Contemporary chronicles and later English historiography

events.certainty_note: Henry VIII's court politics in 1522 are documented in royal records and contemporary accounts.
cross_references: Led to the formal break with Rome (1526 onwards)
Related to the rise of Anne Boleyn

events.title: Cortés consolidates control in Mexico
events.region: Spanish conquest of Mexico / Aztec Empire
events.description: In 1522, Hernán Cortés was consolidating Spanish control over Mexico following the conquest of the Aztec Empire in 1521. Cortés was establishing colonial institutions, distributing encomiendas (land grants with indigenous labor), and initiating the process of converting the indigenous population to Christianity. The year 1522 marks the transition from military conquest to colonial administration.
key_figures: Indigenous leaders and survivors of the conquest

sources.name: Cortés's letters to the Spanish Crown
Spanish colonial records and administrative documents
Indigenous codices and oral traditions (recorded later)

events.certainty_note: The general process of consolidation is documented in Spanish sources, though the specific date of 1522 is somewhat conventional; the process was gradual and extended over several years.
cross_references: Follows from the conquest of the Aztec Empire (1521)
Led to the establishment of New Spain as a Spanish colonial territory

events.title: Reformation spreads in Switzerland
events.description: In 1522, the Protestant Reformation began to spread in Switzerland, particularly in Zurich under the leadership of Ulrich Zwingli. Zwingli's reforms, which emphasized the authority of Scripture and the elimination of Catholic practices, were gaining support among the urban population and some cantons. The Reformation in Switzerland would lead to religious conflict and the division of the Swiss Confederation into Catholic and Protestant regions.

sources.name: Zwingli's writings and correspondence
Swiss city council records and ecclesiastical documents
Later Protestant and Catholic historiography

events.certainty_note: The beginning of the Reformation in Switzerland is dated to the early 1520s, with 1522 being a conventional date for the start of the process, though the exact chronology varies.
cross_references: Part of the broader Protestant Reformation in Northern Europe
Led to religious conflict in Switzerland (1529 onwards)

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Magellan's expedition's arrival in the Philippines is sometimes given as 1521 rather than 1522, with the expedition reaching the Philippines in March 1521 and Magellan dying in April 1521. The continued exploration of the Philippines and the Spice Islands occurred in 1522 under Juan Sebastián Elcano's command. The beginning of the Reformation in Switzerland is sometimes dated to 1523 or 1524 rather than 1522, depending on which specific events are considered the 'beginning.' Babur's campaigns in India in 1522 are mentioned in his memoirs but are not always clearly dated to this specific year.
historiographic_note: 1522 is well-documented in European sources due to the abundance of written records, official chronicles, and diplomatic correspondence. The siege of Rhodes and Magellan's expedition are extensively covered in contemporary sources. Ottoman sources are available for the siege of Rhodes. Chinese imperial records are systematic and comprehensive. The main bias is toward elite political and military events; economic, social, and cultural history of non-elite populations is underrepresented. Indigenous American perspectives on Spanish conquest are largely absent from surviving records. The historical record is heavily weighted toward literate, centralized states.

graph_edges.note: The Ottoman conquest of Rhodes in 1522 led to the Knights Hospitaller's establishment in Malta
The enforcement of the Edict of Worms in 1522 led to the Diet of Nuremberg in 1523
Magellan's expedition's arrival in the Philippines in 1522 led to Spanish colonization of the Philippines
Babur's campaigns in 1522 led to the Battle of Panipat and the establishment of the Mughal Empire in 1526
